content: When applying thrust to a rotatable ship/character, compute velocity from its facing angle: vx = Math.cos(angle) * speed, vy = Math.sin(angle) * speed. NEVER use plain world-axis dx/dy. Canvas y grows downward, so 'forward at angle 0' means +x with no y change; rotating CCW by π/2 (-Math.PI/2 from +x) points up.

Asteroids must be irregular polygons, not perfect circles. Pre-generate ≥8 vertices at angles 0..2π with per-vertex jitter of radius * (0.7..1.3); store the offsets per asteroid and draw with ctx.beginPath / lineTo / closePath. Drawing arc(0,0,r,0,2π) ships a circle and feels wrong.

Animation requires BOTH (a) a frame() function whose final statement is requestAnimationFrame(frame), and (b) at least one initial requestAnimationFrame(frame) call to kick the loop off. Missing either leaves a static canvas — a common silent failure.

Keyboard input MUST use e.code values like 'ArrowLeft', 'KeyW', 'Space'. Don't use e.key (varies with keyboard layout) or e.keyCode (deprecated, missing on some browsers). Mapping: {ArrowUp:'up', ArrowDown:'down', ArrowLeft:'left', ArrowRight:'right', KeyW:'up', KeyS:'down', KeyA:'left', KeyD:'right', Space:'fire'}.

Arrow keys and Space scroll the page by default and steal focus. Inside keydown, call e.preventDefault() for any code in your game key set. Attach the listener to window with {passive:false} so preventDefault actually takes effect.

Attach keydown/keyup listeners to window (or document), NOT to the <canvas> element. Canvas isn't focused by default and won't receive keys. If you really need canvas focus, set tabIndex=0 and call .focus() after creation.

Add window.addEventListener('blur', () => for (k in keys) keys[k]=false) to clear held keys on focus loss; otherwise Alt-Tab leaves the player thrusting forever. Same on document.addEventListener('visibilitychange', ...) when hidden.

Use delta-time physics: dt = Math.min(0.05, (now - last)/1000); last = now. Multiply movement by dt so speed is frame-rate independent. Cap dt to ~0.05s before stepping — without the cap, tab-switch triggers a 5+ second dt and objects tunnel through everything (the 'spiral of death').

On retina/HiDPI displays, the canvas is blurry without DPR scaling. Set: const dpr = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio||1, 2); cvs.width = cssW * dpr; cvs.height = cssH * dpr; cvs.style.width = cssW + 'px'; cvs.style.height = cssH + 'px'; ctx.setTransform(dpr,0,0,dpr,0,0). Re-apply on 'resize'.

Score and controls must be visible at all times. Either render a #hud div with position:fixed (top-left) and update its text from the game state, or fillText('Score: '+score, 12, 24) every frame inside draw(). A game without a visible score is judged broken even if it works.

There MUST be a way to lose AND a way to restart. Use a state machine with state.over flag; show a modal on game over; bind Space or a button to reset the state and restart the loop. Cancel any pending animation frames on restart.

Image loading is async: drawImage before an Image has loaded paints nothing. Do NOT block requestAnimationFrame on every decode; start RAF immediately so the canvas/input stay alive, call loadAssets() in the background, store each Image in ASSETS when decode/onload completes, set window._assetsReady = true when all decodes finish, and draw a clearly labeled fallback only for that missing asset until it loads. This prevents one failed PNG from freezing the whole game.

For mobile/touch, bind pointerdown/pointermove/pointerup OR touchstart/touchmove/touchend (with preventDefault) to the same action handlers as keys. Use Pointer Events when possible — they unify mouse+touch+pen and avoid the touchstart→mousedown double-fire.
On document.visibilitychange when document.hidden, pause the loop AND reset 'last' time on resume. Without this, dt explodes to seconds when returning to the tab and physics fly off.
JavaScript modulo on negative numbers returns a negative result: (-1) % 800 === -1, not 799. For wrap-around use ((x % w) + w) % w.

Drawing order matters. Inside draw(): clear → background → entities → effects/particles → HUD overlay. Don't draw HUD inside the entity loop or it will be obscured by sprites drawn later in the same loop.

Wrap the frame() body in try/catch that logs to console.error. One uncaught exception inside requestAnimationFrame silently stops the loop with no visible error in the page — the game looks frozen with no diagnosis.

On restart, cancel pending animation frames (cancelAnimationFrame) and don't re-add the same event listeners — without cleanup, listeners stack and each restart doubles input speed. Prefer mutating in-place over re-binding.

Snake/grid games move in fixed-cell ticks, not free RAF motion. Use an accumulator: tickAccum += dt; if (tickAccum >= TICK) { step(); tickAccum -= TICK; }. The head must advance EXACTLY one cell per step; turning sets the next direction, applied at the next step.

Breakout's ball must launch with NON-ZERO dy. A common bug is dy=0 on launch — ball travels horizontally forever, paddle never sees it. Initial state: dx = ±2, dy = -3 (negative = up). On paddle hit, mirror dy and bias dx by hit-position offset.

Output is a SINGLE .html file containing inline <style> and <script>. CDN libraries via <script src='https://...'> are allowed (Phaser, three.js, kontra). No bundlers, no node_modules, no separate .js or .css files.
Don't read/write localStorage on first load without a feature-detect — some headless contexts throw SecurityError. try { localStorage.getItem('x') } catch (_) {} before relying on it.
If the HUD is a positioned div over the canvas, set pointer-events:none on it so clicks/touches pass through to the game. Otherwise the HUD silently swallows input.
Browsers block autoplay until a user gesture. If using AudioContext, create it on first keydown/click: if (!audio) { audio = new (window.AudioContext||...)(); } audio.resume().

When loading Phaser/three.js via CDN, place the <script src='...'> in <head> with `defer`, and put your game code in a <script> AFTER the body — otherwise the library may not exist when your script runs.

For UI-style requests (todo list, calculator, tic-tac-toe, word games) prefer DOM elements over canvas. Use <button>, <input>, <ul>, addEventListener('click', ...). Reserve canvas for games that genuinely need per-pixel rendering or smooth animation.

For axis-aligned bounding boxes, collision is: a.x < b.x+b.w && a.x+a.w > b.x && a.y < b.y+b.h && a.y+a.h > b.y. Always check ALL FOUR conditions; getting one direction wrong produces ghost-collisions (objects pass through one side only).
Circle-circle collision: const dx=a.x-b.x, dy=a.y-b.y, rsum=a.r+b.r; if (dx*dx + dy*dy < rsum*rsum) { hit }. Use squared distance — avoids sqrt in the hot loop.
Bullets/projectiles: pre-allocate a pool and reuse via an active flag, instead of new Bullet()/array.push every fire. Garbage collection in the middle of a frame causes hitches visible as stutter.

When the user provides a seed file, PREFER patches over a full rewrite. A wholesale rewrite loses structural choices the user may care about (variable names, layout, styling). Patches keep their work intact and only change what the goal demands.

For tile-grid levels, PLACE spawn/exit/enemies/pickups by SCANNING empty cells in code — never hand-verify coordinates in your reply. Snippet: function pickEmpty(map){ while(true){ const x=1+Math.floor(Math.random()*(W-2)), y=1+Math.floor(Math.random()*(H-2)); if(map[y][x]===0) return {x:x+0.5,y:y+0.5}; } }. Enumerating 'row 9 col 3 = wall!' in <think> burns thousands of tokens and often never reaches <html_file>.

3D wireframe perspective on 2D canvas (Battlezone-style): (1) Rotate to camera space: dx=x-px, dz=z-pz; rx=dx*cos(-θ)-dz*sin(-θ); rz=dx*sin(-θ)+dz*cos(-θ). (2) Project: if(rz<=0.1) return; screenX=cx+(rx/rz)*fov; screenY=cy-(y/rz)*fov. (3) Sort objects by descending rz (painter's algorithm) so closer geometry draws on top; connect with beginPath/moveTo/lineTo/stroke. MOVEMENT on the xz plane uses the SAME yaw as projection: forward `x += sin(yaw)*speed; z += cos(yaw)*speed` (+cos on z, NOT three.js `-cos`). Radar/minimap blips use the same sin/cos pair — see wireframe-fps-movement-vectors and wireframe-minimap-radar-yaw-arrow.

For platformers with climbing (e.g. Donkey Kong) and one-way platforms: (1) Ladders: Allow climbing only when entity's center is closely aligned horizontally with the ladder's x position. Set gravity to zero and allow vertical velocity while climbing. (2) One-way platforms: Allow entities to jump up through platforms by checking collisions ONLY when velocity vy is positive (falling) AND the entity's feet (bottom) are above the platform's top edge before the step. Check: if (vy > 0 && feet <= platformTop && feet + vy >= platformTop) { land_on_platform }.

For fighting/action games (e.g. Street Fighter): (1) State Machine: Separate characters into strict states: idle, walking, jumping, crouching, attack-startup, attack-active, attack-recovery, hitstun, knockback. Block input during attacks, hitstun, and knockback. (2) Combat Collision: Do NOT check plain player bounding-box overlaps for attacks. Instead, define 'hurtboxes' (body regions that can be hit) and 'hitboxes' (attack regions, e.g. fist/foot). Check overlap of attacker's active hitbox with defender's hurtbox ONLY during 'attack-active' animation frames.

To project a 3D grid onto an isometric screen coordinate system (e.g. Q-bert style): For a grid coordinate (row, col, height): screenX = centerX + (col - row) * (tileWidth / 2); screenY = centerY + (col + row) * (tileHeight / 2) - height * heightScale. To sort rendering order to prevent overlaps (painter's algorithm), draw entities by sorting from lowest (row + col) to highest. For isometric jumping, animate entity's height with a parabolic curve: height = jumpApex * (4 * t * (1 - t)) where t goes 0..1.

To implement smooth tile-based corner sliding (e.g. Pac-Man) in continuous movement: Allow the player to buffer turns before aligning perfectly with a tile axis. If key is pressed, check if coordinate orthogonal to movement is close to a tile boundary (e.g., within a snap tolerance of 8px). If close, snap coordinate directly to tile center (p.y = Math.floor(p.y/TILE)*TILE) and change velocity. This prevents players from sticking to walls when turning corridors.

For Space Invaders alien wave formations: Store coordinates in a grid matrix or array. Move all aliens horizontally together. Keep track of left-most and right-most active bounds. When either bound touches screen margins, reverse horizontal direction for all aliens and increment their y position downward by one step. To keep tension, increase movement update speed proportionally as the number of remaining aliens decreases (e.g., delay = baseDelay * (activeCount / total)).

To make segment links cleanly follow a leader (e.g. Centipede): Store a history array of the leader's coordinates (e.g., up to 200 points). Each follower segment is assigned a specific history index offset (e.g., segment i reads index i * segmentSpacing from history). On update, push leader's latest coordinate to the front of the history array and trim the tail. This prevents segments from overlapping or separating during turns.

To prevent endless horizontal/vertical bouncing in Breakout: Modify ball bounce angle based on where it strikes the paddle. Calculate offset = (ball.x - paddle.x) / paddle.width (valued -0.5 to +0.5). Determine bounce direction angle: angle = (offset * maxBiasAngle) - Math.PI/2. Set new ball velocities: vx = Math.cos(angle) * speed; vy = Math.sin(angle) * speed (making vy negative/up). This rewards precision hits.

For side-scrolling worlds (e.g. Defender): Create a camera object holding a single horizontal offset: camera.x = player.x - centerX. When rendering, wrap context drawings inside ctx.save() / ctx.translate(-camera.x, -camera.y). Render background details with an offset multiplier (e.g., star.x - camera.x * 0.3) to achieve parallax depth. Automatically clear/recycle bullets and enemies once they move beyond the viewport boundaries (e.g., x < camera.x - 100).

SNES Mode 7 ground projection on 2D canvas (Mario Kart style): for each scanline y below horizon, distance = fov / (y + 1); scaleX = distance / fov; stepX = sin(θ)*scaleX, stepY = -cos(θ)*scaleX. Per pixel along the line: worldX = player.x + cos(θ)*distance + (screenX - cx)*stepX (and matching worldY). Sample track texture, clamp bounds, write to getImageData buffer.

To rotate 2D piece matrices in Tetris: Transpose and reverse the grid array: rotated = matrix[0].map((_, i) => matrix.map(row => row[i]).reverse()). To prevent clipping walls on rotation, implement a 'wall-kick' guard: If the rotated shape collides with side boundaries or locked cells, try shifting its position left or right by 1 or 2 cells. If all shifts still collide, revert rotation. When rows are cleared, slice them out and unshift empty rows of width W to the top of the board.

To prevent players from sticking to walls during diagonal movement (e.g. Gauntlet): Decompose player movement into separate horizontal and vertical steps. Step 1: Apply x movement `p.x += p.vx * dt`, and check collisions. If overlapping a wall boundary, snap x position back and zero out `vx`. Step 2: Apply y movement `p.y += p.vy * dt`, and check collisions. If overlapping, snap y back. This allows the player to slide smoothly along walls.

For shared shared-screen multiplayer viewports (e.g. Gauntlet): Calculate camera center as the average coordinates of all active players: centerX = sum(players.x) / count. Clamp camera coordinate boundaries to global level bounds. Clamping players: Prevent players from leaving screen edges by checking their distance to the clamped camera bounds. If a player coordinates `p.x` leaves `centerX - halfScreenWidth`, clamp their coordinates to the screen margin.

To calculate player velocity from virtual joystick touches: On pointermove, compute offsets: dx = touchX - joystickStartX, dy = touchY - joystickStartY. Find absolute distance and angle: dist = Math.hypot(dx, dy), theta = Math.atan2(dy, dx). If distance exceeds max joystick radius, clamp touch visual coordinates to `maxRadius`. Calculate player velocities: vx = Math.cos(theta) * speed * Math.min(1, dist/maxRadius), and matching vy. This ensures responsive and precise multi-directional mobile steering.

To letterbox a standard canvas for mobile viewports (e.g. iPad/iPhone): On resize, fetch window aspect ratio and scale canvas container styles. Check: scale = Math.min(window.innerWidth / baseW, window.innerHeight / baseH). Set canvas styled width/height: cvs.style.width = (baseW * scale) + 'px'; cvs.style.height = (baseH * scale) + 'px'. Center canvas container with flexbox or fixed absolute positioning. This prevents screen distortion on rotated devices.

For card/board drag-and-drop mechanics (e.g. Solitaire): On pointerdown, test bounding box of all items from top-drawn to bottom. On match, record active item and calculate dragging offset relative to cursor: offsetX = mouseX - item.x. On pointermove, set `item.x = mouseX - offsetX`. On pointerup, check overlaps with valid slots using circle-distance or AABB. If overlapping, snap item coordinate directly to target coordinates, else lerp back to original slot.

To implement discrete grid step movement (e.g. Pokemon/Ultima/Pac-Man): Do NOT move player sprites continuously on arrow holds. Store separate coordinates: gridX/gridY (logical grid) and animX/animY (visual coordinates). When a direction key is pressed and player is idle: set target cells, block input, and flag player as moving. Increment a step-progress fraction: moveProgress += speed * dt; visually interpolate pixelX = (gridX + dir.x*moveProgress) * TILE. When moveProgress >= 1, snap to the next grid cell, unblock input, reset moveProgress = 0. SPEED UNITS — critical: moveProgress is a 0->1 FRACTION of ONE tile, so `speed` here is TILES PER SECOND (~4-8 for a brisk Pac-Man, dt in seconds). Do NOT write `moveProgress += speed * dt / TILE` — dividing a tile-FRACTION by TILE_SIZE (~20-30) makes everything move ~20x too slow, a glacial crawl (observed 2026-05-30 Pac-Man bug: PAC_SPEED*dt/TILE). The `/TILE` only belongs if `speed` is a PIXELS-per-second value being converted to a fraction. Same rule for ghosts/NPCs — use tiles/sec, no stray /TILE.

To bounce projectile circles off wall boundaries (e.g. Bubble Shooter): Upon edge collision check, mirror the horizontal velocity: vx = -vx. To reflect projectile circles off line segments: find normal vector of segment. Velocity reflection formula: R = V - 2 * (V . N) * N. For gravity path forecasting: Plot trajectory coordinates inside a simple loop: nextX = x + vx * t; nextY = y + vy * t + 0.5 * gravity * t*t. Draw forecasted points on canvas as a guide line.

To implement simple grid pathfinding for enemy chasers (e.g. Pac-Man ghosts, Gauntlet ghosts) avoiding walls: Use Breadth-First Search (BFS) to find the shortest path in a 2D tile map. Algorithm: Let queue = [[start]]; let visited = set(start). While queue is not empty, pop first path. Get last cell in path. If it equals target, return first step of path. Otherwise, for each neighboring cell (up, down, left, right), if inside map, not a wall, and not visited: visited.add(neighbor) and push path + [neighbor] to queue. Falls back to straight-line direction vector when target is unreached.

To manage complex enemy behaviors in arcade games: Implement a Finite State Machine (FSM) with states: 'patrol' (walk between spawn nodes), 'alert' (stop and look around when player is near), 'chase' (pathfind towards player coordinates), and 'attack' (trigger attack-frames and pause movement). Transition triggers: distance to player < alertRadius transitions to 'alert', distance < chaseRadius transitions to 'chase', and distance < attackRadius transitions to 'attack'. Use cooldown-ticks or timers to enforce state durations.

Pseudo-3D curved road: for each screen y below horizon, z=fov/(y-horizon); roadX = center + curve*z*z; screenW = baseW/z; draw the segment; scale sprites by 1/z. Rivals: discrete lane x + unique z, sort far→near before draw (random x overwrites). drawImage car sprites; steering shifts playerX while the player sprite stays bottom-center.

To manage smooth sprite animations (running, swinging, hitting) with variable frame rates: Track animation state holding: animTimer, currentFrameIndex, and activeSpriteSheet. On update, increment: `animTimer += dt`. If `animTimer >= 1 / fps`: reset timer `animTimer -= 1 / fps` and advance frame `currentFrameIndex = (currentFrameIndex + 1) % totalFrames`. When rendering, draw image using clipping coordinates: `ctx.drawImage(sheet, currentFrameIndex * frameW, 0, frameW, frameH, x, y, w, h)`. This keeps visual cycles consistent and speed-independent.

To implement responsive input buffering for fighting or action games (e.g. Street Fighter, platformers): Create a buffer queue array to store recent inputs with timestamps: `inputBuffer.push({ key, t: now })`. On update, filter out old actions: `inputBuffer = inputBuffer.filter(item => now - item.t < bufferWindowSeconds)` (window is typically 0.15 to 0.25 seconds). When a player exits recovery states, hitstun, or lands on the ground: check if any valid command key (e.g., 'jump' or 'attack') exists in the active buffer. If found, consume it immediately: execute action and clear buffer.

To render smooth, glowing, or animated health and ammo meters on canvas: Draw a background bar: `ctx.fillStyle = '#300'; ctx.fillRect(x, y, maxW, h);`. Linearly interpolate visible value to actual value for a smooth animated fill effect: `visibleVal += (actualVal - visibleVal) * 0.1` (or `dt * speed`). Draw filled bar: `ctx.fillStyle = '#f33'; ctx.fillRect(x, y, (visibleVal / maxVal) * maxW, h);`. Add a glossy stroke outline and thin vertical interval lines to make it look clean and highly readable.

Acceptance probes look up state via window.gameState, window.state, or window.game.reset() — un-exposed state fails probes even when the game works. Always end init with: window.gameState = state; window.game = { reset: () => resetGame() }. Score, player position, currentPlayer, and any reset entry point must be reachable from a probe in ≤3 lines.

Automated acceptance probes typically evaluate window.gameState after a ~3-second warmup. Expose the state object synchronously before any async asset loading or initialization callbacks. Remove or minimize startup delays (ready timers, intro screens, loading gates) so gameplay begins immediately and probes can verify score increases or entity positions within the evaluation window.

Turn-based board UIs use a two-click commit: state.selected = null at start; clicking your own piece sets selected and computes legalMoves(); clicking a legal target calls applyMove() then state.currentPlayer = (currentPlayer === 'W' ? 'B' : 'W'). Block input during animations or AI thinking. Highlight the selected cell + legal targets so the user knows what's clickable.

Store a 2D board as board[r][c] with r=row=y-index, c=col=x-index — NEVER mix (x,y) and (r,c) in the same function. Render with: for(let r=0;r<SIZE;r++) for(let c=0;c<SIZE;c++) { const piece = board[r][c]; drawAt(c*TILE, r*TILE, piece); }. Always (row, col) order in code; (col*TILE, row*TILE) only at the draw boundary.

Pointer -> board cell: const rect = cvs.getBoundingClientRect(); const mx = e.clientX - rect.left, my = e.clientY - rect.top; const c = Math.floor(mx / TILE), r = Math.floor(my / TILE); if (r < 0 || r >= SIZE || c < 0 || c >= SIZE) return; — rejects clicks outside the board. Use pointerdown (unified mouse + touch + pen) and call e.preventDefault() if the page scrolls.

ONLY when NO generated sprite PNGs exist for board pieces: if rendering pieces with Unicode symbols (e.g., ♔♕♖♗♘♙ for white, ♚♛♜♝♞♟ for black), explicitly set CSS color classes because Unicode glyphs inherit the current text color. Black pieces will appear invisible or white on light backgrounds without `color: #000` or a dark class. Apply via: `.piece-white { color: #fff; text-shadow: 0 0 2px #000; } .piece-black { color: #000; }` to ensure contrast on both light and dark squares. When generated sprites exist, draw pieces with drawImage — do NOT use Unicode fillText instead of sprites.

Standard chess board orientation places Black pieces on rank 0 (top row when rendering row 0 at the visual top) and White pieces on rank 7 (bottom row). Initialize with: `board[0] = ['♜','♞','♝','♛','♚','♝','♞','♜']` and `board[7] = ['♖','♘','♗','♕','♔','♗','♘','♖']`. This convention ensures the AI (Black) starts at the top and the human (White) at the bottom, matching FIDE display standards.

For board/grid games, animate piece movement by spawning a temporary absolutely-positioned overlay element at the source cell, applying a CSS transition to slide it to the target cell's coordinates, and removing it after the transition ends before re-rendering the board. This avoids layout thrashing and keeps the underlying grid structure stable during the animation.

The test harness validates interactivity by pressing keyboard keys and checking for pixel changes. For click-driven games (chess, board games, UI menus), implement a keyboard navigation fallback (e.g., arrow keys move a visible cursor, Enter/Space confirms selection) to satisfy the input_responsive probe without altering the primary mouse controls. This fallback is for the harness only — STILL write 'mouse click' (not 'arrow keys') in your <criteria> so the input-modality check treats the game as pointer-primary; otherwise the keyboard-fallback listeners make the harness expect keyboard-primary movement and a board cursor that only moves selection (not a player x/y) trips a false PLAYER-STUCK / input-dead gate.

When iterating over dynamically generated lists for rendering (e.g., legalTargets, highlights, particles), always guard against missing or undefined properties before accessing them. Use early returns like `if (!entry || entry.r === undefined) continue;` to prevent TypeError crashes in the draw loop that freeze the game.

In minimax or alpha-beta AI move generation, always declare move arrays with let instead of const (e.g., let moves = generateMoves(...)). Move ordering, filtering, or iterative deepening frequently reassigns the variable, and using const causes TypeError: Assignment to constant variable crashes that silently break AI turns and stall game state.

When building complex single-file games with AI, full rule sets, and animations, structure the code with clear separation: constants, state, utility functions, game logic, rendering, and input handling. Initialize the requestAnimationFrame loop at the very end of the script, outside any conditional blocks, and add a console.log startup check to verify execution. This prevents structural syntax errors from silently breaking the game.

Wrap all game logic in a single-file HTML project inside an IIFE or `main()` function call to prevent 'Illegal return statement' errors and ensure proper variable scoping. Structure as: `function main() { /* all game code */ } main();` at the bottom of the script. This catches stray returns, prevents global namespace pollution, and makes initialization order explicit.

Generated sprites should not delay the RAF kickoff. Start requestAnimationFrame(frame) immediately, kick off loadAssets() in the background, and make draw() tolerate missing images with small explicit MISSING placeholders. Never wait for Promise.all(images.map(decode)) before starting the loop: one failed/stalled asset creates a blank canvas and input_responsive failure. When assets appear, sprite()/drawImage replaces the fallback automatically — but ONLY if sprite() caches the resolved Image object, NOT a readiness verdict. Never cache a miss while the Image is still decoding (complete===false / naturalWidth===0): that makes the miss permanent and is the #1 'reload a few times and the art shows up' bug. Cache null only for a key that matches no asset file at all, and check img.complete && img.naturalWidth > 0 at DRAW time so a matched-but-decoding sprite draws the placeholder this frame and the real art the frame after it decodes.

When implementing multiple enemy entities with different chase behaviors, give each entity a unique targeting function rather than a single shared AI. For example, in Pac-Man: Blinky targets Pac-Man's current tile, Pinky targets 4 tiles ahead, Inky uses a vector offset from Blinky's position, Clyde switches between chase and scatter based on distance. Store each entity's targeting logic as a method or function reference, then call it during the chase mode to compute the target tile for pathfinding.

For power-up mechanics that temporarily change entity behavior, use a countdown timer that triggers state transitions. Store the vulnerability duration as frames (e.g., frightenedTimer = 600 for ~10 seconds at 60fps). Each frame, decrement the timer and check thresholds: when timer crosses specific values, flash the entity (toggle appearance) to warn of impending state change. When timer reaches zero, restore normal behavior. This pattern works for any temporary buff/debuff affecting enemy AI or collision rules.

Represent maze layouts as a 2D array of characters where each character maps to a tile type (e.g., '#' for wall, '.' for dot, 'o' for power pellet, ' ' for empty). Parse this array at initialization to build the game grid, placing walls, collectibles, and spawn points. This approach makes level design readable and editable, and works for any tile-based game. Store the parsed grid as a flat array or 2D array for O(1) tile lookups during collision detection.

Automated visual harnesses flag a static canvas as 'frozen'/'unresponsive' when pixels are identical across the observation window. This bites SINGLE-CHARACTER FIGHTING / ANIMATION / showcase / martial-artist games that sit on a STATIC IDLE sprite until the player presses a key (punch/kick/jump/run/duck/fireball) — the idle is correct but reads as frozen. ALWAYS give the idle a continuous, subtle ambient animation (per-entity idle bob/breathing, a 2-frame idle cycle, pulsing UI, or background parallax) so there is frame-to-frame pixel delta even while waiting for input. (The harness now downgrades a frozen-at-idle canvas to a non-blocking warning when the input test proves keys change the canvas — but a real idle animation looks better and silences it.)

three.js first-person mouse-look + WASD/arrow movement ONLY — do NOT use this bullet for 2D canvas wireframe Battlezone/tank games (see wireframe-fps-movement-vectors). INVARIANT: movement forward must match camera forward on the xz plane — see 3d-navigation-modality-invariants. PREFERRED (always matches camera): build a local move vector, apply camera quaternion, zero Y: `const move = new THREE.Vector3(); if (keys.up) move.z -= 1; if (keys.down) move.z += 1; if (keys.left) move.x -= 1; if (keys.right) move.x += 1; move.normalize().multiplyScalar(speed*dt).applyQuaternion(camera.quaternion); move.y = 0; camera.position.add(move); state.player.x = camera.position.x; state.player.z = camera.position.z;`. Sync yaw for probes: `state.player.yaw = camera.rotation.y`. Pointer-lock: `yaw -= movementX * sensitivity` (SUBTRACT); pitch: `pitch -= movementY * sensitivity`; clamp pitch. Camera: `camera.rotation.order = 'YXZ'; camera.rotation.y = yaw; camera.rotation.x = pitch`. MANUAL FALLBACK (only if not using Vector3/quaternion): `fx = -Math.sin(yaw); fz = -Math.cos(yaw); rx = -Math.cos(yaw); rz = Math.sin(yaw)` — NEVER use `fx = +Math.sin(yaw)` with `camera.rotation.y = yaw`; three.js looks along (-sin,-cos) on xz so +sin on X walks opposite the gun on some facings. Minimap/fire rays reuse the SAME forward — see fps-minimap-radar-yaw-arrow.

First-person sprites: enemies / pickups (billboards) + first-person weapon view. BILLBOARD sprites face the camera ONLY around Y so they don't tilt: `sprite.lookAt(camera.position.x, sprite.position.y, camera.position.z)` (note `sprite.position.y`, NOT `camera.position.y`, to lock pitch). three.js `THREE.Sprite` auto-faces by default, but you still need `material.depthWrite = false` if the PNG has alpha — otherwise overlapping sprites punch holes in the depth buffer and you see through them to the skybox. WEAPON VIEW (first-person gun in lower screen): treat as a SCREEN-SPACE overlay — an HTML `` positioned with CSS, OR a `THREE.Sprite` parented to the camera, NOT a world-space mesh that moves with the player. The PNG's MUZZLE must already point UP in the source image — if it points sideways, the gun visibly aims away from the crosshair regardless of any code rotation. The classic FPS convention is a STATIC bottom-center weapon overlay + a STATIC screen-center crosshair, with the camera doing all the aiming; do NOT rotate the gun PNG to 'aim' at the crosshair, and do NOT swap drawImage for procedural ctx drawing in a mid-session fix — that loses the asset path and regresses. To FLIP a weapon sprite that exported facing the wrong way, mirror it ONCE at draw time — 2D canvas: `ctx.save(); ctx.translate(x+w, 0); ctx.scale(-1, 1); ctx.drawImage(img, -x, y, w, h); ctx.restore();`; three.js material: `material.map.repeat.x = -1; material.map.offset.x = 1; material.needsUpdate = true;`. Either is preferred over regenerating the asset.

If you draw entities via `ctx.drawImage(sprite, x, y, w, h)` AND expose entity position as `state.entity.x = x`, the harness state-vs-render check samples pixel (x, y) — the sprite's TOP-LEFT CORNER. Generated sprites (Z-Image-Turbo with `transparent background` prompts) have ~10-20% transparent padding around the figure, so the corner samples 100% background → ENTITY-NOT-RENDERED fires even though the entity IS drawn. Two fixes: (a) treat state.x/y as the CENTER and draw with `ctx.drawImage(sprite, x - w/2, y - h/2, w, h)`; or (b) expose `state.entity.cx = x + w/2; cy = y + h/2` so the harness samples the visible center.

Cascading top→bottom hazards on a multi-floor stage (barrels on girders, ingredients on platforms, logs on ledges — any theme) need a RESPWN LOOP, not a one-shot spawn. On sloped floors: ROLL along the current surface (snap y to the platform while moving) and only DROP at the intentional low-end / gap onto the next floor — then reverse direction. Do NOT free-fall off the first floor into empty air. Each frame: advance on surface or tumble to next floor; if off the bottom of the playfield, REMOVE and spawn a fresh one at the top spawner. Code shape: `for (let i = hazards.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) { update(hazards[i]); if (offCanvas(hazards[i])) { hazards.splice(i, 1); } } if (now - lastSpawn > SPAWN_INTERVAL) { spawnHazard(); lastSpawn = now; }`. Probe: hazards still present AND totalSpawned grew after N seconds.

In a vertical platformer with ladders connecting multiple floors, the ladder's yTop and yBot MUST match the y-coordinate of an actual floor / girder. Hardcoding pixel offsets ('yTop: 460, yBot: 540') drifts: any floor adjustment leaves ladders floating or buried. Pattern: derive ladder bounds from the platform list, never hardcode pixel y-values: `const platforms = [{y: 560}, {y: 460}, {y: 360}, ...]; const ladders = [{x: 80, yTop: platforms[1].y, yBot: platforms[0].y}, ...];`. Player climb logic: when player is on a ladder and player.y <= ladder.yTop, snap to the upper platform (player.y = ladder.yTop - playerHeight). `/vlm-critique` checklist `canvas-vertical-platformer` checks every visible ladder touches BOTH a floor above AND a floor below — failure here means the ladder is floating.

When the goal lists many features (renderer + enemies + animation + HUD + pickups + sounds + win/lose + minimap + ...), the FIRST <html_file> must ship the SMALLEST playable core: renderer + ONE input + ONE moving entity. Defer enemies, pickups, animation frames, HUD details, sound wiring, win/lose to subsequent <patch> turns. Why: trying to emit every feature in iter 1 fills the context with one huge <html_file>, then any fix prompt re-inlines that file plus history and leaves the model no token budget to write a corrected version — output truncates, parser rejects, the session locks into an identical-reply loop. How to apply: count distinct mechanics in your <plan>. If more than ~5, write <criteria> for only the first 2 (renderer works, one input has visible effect) and put the rest in <todos> as `[ ]` items to fulfill on later turns. The harness will accept the small first build and the patch loop will add features one at a time without context collapse.

When the inlined CURRENT FILE block in your prompt has grown large (the file plus prior turns approaches your model's context window), STOP emitting <html_file> and STOP re-emitting <probes> / <assets> / <sounds>. Emit a single small <patch> with the minimum unique SEARCH anchor — 3 to 5 lines is usually enough. Re-emitting the full file when context is tight will silently truncate the stream mid-output and produce an unclosed <html_file> the parser cannot extract. Why: the agent loop will keep sending the same fallback when it can't parse your reply, and you will keep emitting the same broken thing — a dead identical-reply loop that burns the rest of the iteration budget. How to apply: if you see a 'CONTEXT IS FULL' or 'context_pressure_warning' hint from the harness, switch to <patch> immediately. As a heuristic, once any <html_file> has appeared in prior turns and the file is >20KB, default to <patch> for the rest of the session and only use <html_file> if a structural change truly cannot be expressed as a patch.

Expose game state under BOTH `window.state` AND `window.gameState` (one line at the end of init: `window.gameState = window.state = state;`). Acceptance probes you write often reference `gameState.health` / `gameState.player.x` / `gameState.score` — if you only expose one name, those probes silently return undefined and your iter scores 0 / N even when the game runs perfectly. Why: in mid-tier-model traces the planner reaches for `gameState` as the conventional name in <probes> while the coder reaches for `state` as the conventional name in code; the harness then reports false negatives that the model misreads as real bugs and starts patching working code. How to apply: at the very end of your IIFE, immediately after the requestAnimationFrame kickoff, add the dual alias. Also alias `window.game = window.game || { reset: () => resetGame() }` so probes calling `game.reset()` work. The probe_lint_postbuild check stops flagging unassigned-property reads once this is in place.

When user feedback says 'flip X 180' or 'reverse X direction' or 'invert X' for a specific named state (e.g. punch, kick, fireball, gun, sprite), the MINIMUM change is to wrap that ONE draw call in `ctx.save(); ctx.translate(x + w/2, y + h/2); ctx.scale(-1, 1); ctx.drawImage(sprite, -w/2, -h/2, w, h); ctx.restore();`. Do NOT restructure existing facing-direction state machines, do NOT touch the `if (state === 'punch')` conditional shape, do NOT modify the flip code for OTHER states. The user wants a per-state visual mirror, not a structural change to how facing works. If you find yourself thinking 'I should make this exclusive of the facing flip,' you are overstepping — emit just the wrapper.

When user feedback contains 'only', 'just', 'nothing else', 'no other changes', 'do not change', 'do not touch', 'one small change' — your patch must touch ONLY the named identifier or function. If you cannot make the change without touching shared code, emit a <question> asking the user to clarify whether the shared change is OK, instead of overstepping. The model often instinctively wants to 'improve' adjacent code while it's there. Resist. The user asked for narrow; ship narrow.

When asked to ADD a new feature (e.g. 'add fireball flying', 'add a powerup', 'add second player'), the patch must NOT modify existing working functions unless the new feature literally requires it. Add new functions, insert new event handlers, wire a new state path — but do not refactor neighboring code 'while you're there.' Especially do not delete or rewrite lines from a previous user-requested fix (a `ctx.scale`, an offset, a state-machine guard) — those are user-locked invariants. Adding a feature is a strict superset operation: more code in, no code out.

If a previous turn shipped an explicit user-requested fix and that turn passed, treat the changed lines as LOCKED INVARIANTS. Common shapes: a `ctx.scale(-1, 1)` mirror for a state, an offset constant the user dialed in ('75 pixels to the right'), a state-machine guard ('don't fire while paused'), a position constant ('player starts at x=100'). If your current patch would delete or modify any of these, emit a <question> first — the user fixed that on purpose; you do not silently revert it just because you think the structure could be cleaner.

Scoped user feedback ('only X', 'just Y', 'no other changes') sets a hard patch budget: ONE <patch> block, at most 3 SEARCH/REPLACE hunks, at most ~8 lines changed total. If your fix doesn't fit that budget, the fix is wrong-shaped — try a different approach (e.g. a single ctx wrapper instead of restructuring a function), or emit a <question> asking the user to widen the scope. Multi-hunk multi-region patches under a scope-lock are an overstep regardless of intent.

A `/vlm-critique` finding ('right character facing forward/right, not left' / 'attack not visible' / 'characters clustered on left') is INTERPRETATION from a screenshot, not ground truth. When the critic's complaint contradicts an explicit previous user fix on the same axis (orientation, position, scale, color), TRUST THE USER. The critic may be misreading the screenshot, or the recipe may be applying to a game shape it doesn't fit. Do NOT undo a previous user-explicit `ctx.scale(-1, 1)`, offset, or state-machine change because the critic complained — instead, address the critic's complaint with the MINIMUM ADDITIONAL change that doesn't disturb the prior fix. If the two seem irreconcilable, emit a <question>.

Animation = a SERIES of frames showing the SAME character in different POSES (idle, walk, punch, kick), cycled at runtime. Generate each pose as TXT2IMG with the SAME detailed character+style description and only the POSE clause changed ('right arm fully extended forward, fist out' / 'leg raised high'). The pipeline uses a FIXED seed (42), so the same description renders the SAME character in the new pose. PROVEN 2026-05-30 (see animation_ab/ A/B): img2img / from_image CANNOT change a pose — at guidance_scale=0 it stays locked to the idle init image at EVERY strength, with BOTH Z-Image and SD-Turbo (SD-Turbo gave byte-identical output at 0.55/0.75/0.90). Only txt2img moves the limbs. The harness now auto-renders any from_image frame as txt2img by merging the parent's character prompt with this frame's pose clause, so emitting from_image still works — but the real mechanism is txt2img + shared description + fixed seed. Delta-vs-idle is NOT a pose check (a style-drifted idle scores 0.3+ with no punch); `/vlm-critique` confirms the pose reads. Never draw the limb in code. For 'high resolution / best graphics' goals describe DETAILED art, not blocky 8-bit pixel-art. Vehicle / plane bank+tilt poses need EXTREME visible asymmetry ('hard left bank, left wing tip toward camera, right wing raised') — vague 'banking left' + idle 'facing straight up' fights itself and frames stay ~identical to idle.

An attack/ability must be ANIMATED, not a single static pose held for the whole move. 'Animated' means a SEQUENCE of >=2 distinct frames (windup -> strike -> recover) OR continuous motion (translate/rotate/scale) DURING the active window - and the pose must visibly differ from idle (extended arm for a punch, raised leg for a kick, a spawned projectile for a fireball). The harness hard-fails a move that renders one frozen frame: a STATIC-ACTION soft warning fires when a responsive action key produces no in-hold canvas motion, and it BLOCKS <done/>. It also warns when a from_image frame came out near-identical to its parent (ASSET SANITY WARNING). PREFERRED FIX: generate >=2 sprite frames per attack via from_image from the idle/base pose and CYCLE them across the active window so the motion follows the standing character. Real users repeatedly REJECT limbs/shapes drawn in code on top of the sprite ('code objects') - use a procedural overlay only as a last resort when the art truly will not comply, never as the first move. Always align the hit-window timing to the frames where the pose is actually extended.

When a sprite is mirrored by a `facing` value (e.g. `ctx.scale(-1,1)` when facing===-1), the flip must be relative to each sprite's NATIVE drawn orientation. If RYU art is drawn facing right and KEN art is drawn facing left, a single `flip = facing===-1` rule will point one of them backwards. Track each character's native facing and flip = (facing !== native). Verify the RENDERED result, not just the `facing` state variable: a probe that checks `p1.facing` can pass while the punch/kick/walk sprite visibly points AWAY from the opponent. After any facing change, confirm attacks and walk animations point toward the opponent for BOTH characters.

Before emitting <done/> or <confirm_done/>, re-read the user's MOST RECENT feedback and confirm EVERY distinct item in it is (a) implemented AND (b) still works in the current file - not just the first or easiest one. Multi-part feedback like 'animate the kick AND add a CPU opponent' is two items; shipping one and dropping the other is the most common complaint. When you add a requested feature, also add or refresh a <probe> that verifies it (e.g. 'in CPU mode the opponent's state changes with no player input'), so a later patch cannot silently regress it - a feature added one turn and gone two turns later is a classic failure. If you cannot satisfy an item, say so explicitly rather than going silent on it.

Attack limb points AWAY from the opponent: fix in CODE with one EXTRA ctx.scale(-1,1) for that action only — do not regenerate art or wait for a VLM. Author kick/punch as limb EXTENDED FORWARD to the RIGHT; hitboxes use the same facing sign as the flip. Prefer code flip over `/vlm-critique` / art regen.

For a VISUAL bug in a generated sprite — wrong DIRECTION (facing/limb backward), wrong POSITION, wrong SIZE, slight misplacement — prefer a MINOR CODE transform over REGENERATING the asset: a horizontal/vertical flip (ctx.scale(-1,1) / (1,-1)), a rotation (ctx.rotate), an offset (translate), or a scale factor. Code fixes are instant, deterministic, and reversible; regeneration costs a model call, is slow, and the image generator is unreliable at honoring direction/position/size. Use `/vlm-critique` or the user's feedback to pinpoint exactly what looks wrong, then make the SMALLEST code change that fixes it. Only regenerate when the asset's CONTENT (what it depicts) is wrong — never just to re-orient, resize, or reposition it.

Draw entity sprites LARGE and readable on canvas — not tiny clutter. Fighters/platformer heroes: roughly 35-50% of CANVAS HEIGHT (~0.4*canvas.height), feet on the ground line, clearly visible near mid-stage. Shooters/arcade: player ship/cannon ~48-96px tall; main enemies, invaders, gems, blocks, and props large enough to read at a glance. Tiny sprites hide animation from harness action-frame capture and `/vlm-critique`. Preserve aspect ratio; prefer fewer larger sprites over a field of dots. Versus fighters: start a comfortable distance apart, facing each other — not clustered in one corner.

Pac-Man / maze games: DO NOT author the maze array row-by-row from scratch — weak models fall into a REPETITION LOOP emitting near-identical rows (e.g. '0200000...' x25) until the stream stalls/truncates, leaving a degenerate all-open maze. Instead COPY this compact, valid, fully-connected starter maze (every corridor cell is reachable; '#'=wall, '.'=corridor) and extend it only if you must, keeping rows EQUAL length: const MAZE=['#############','#...........#','#.#.#.#.#.#.#','#...........#','#.#.#.#.#.#.#','#...........#','#############']; Spawn Pac-Man and EACH ghost on a '.' corridor cell — scan MAZE for a '.' and place them there, never on a '#' or outside the array bounds (this prevents the 'starts outside the maze / inside a wall' bug). Use TILE coordinates for logic (tx,ty) and derive pixels at draw time (px=tx*TILE, py=ty*TILE) so wall checks and rendering use the SAME units — mixing pixel and tile units is the #1 cause of 'walks through walls'. Move only onto a non-wall cell: check MAZE[nextTy][nextTx] !== '#' BEFORE applying the move, for BOTH Pac-Man and ghosts. For smooth pixel movement, only allow a turn when the entity is centered on a tile (snap to the grid at cell centers) so it never gets stuck mid-corridor.

Pac-Man / maze games fail in three classic ways — fix all at build time. (1) SPAWN ON A CORRIDOR: never hardcode a start cell that might be a wall. Scan the maze 2D array for a corridor cell (0 or '.') and place Pac-Man AND every ghost there; convert tile->pixel consistently (px = col*TILE + TILE/2). (2) ONE COORDINATE CONVENTION: mixing a PIXEL position (player.x=140) with a TILE-indexed maze[ty][tx] is the #1 cause of 'spawns in a wall', 'stuck and cannot move', and 'walks through walls'. Pick tile coords (tx,ty) and do movement/collision in tile space, OR pixel coords and convert with Math.floor(x/TILE) for every wall check — never mix. (3) WALL CHECK BEFORE MOVING: compute the target cell in the pressed direction and only move if maze[targetRow][targetCol] is NOT a wall; align to tile centers so turns only happen at intersections (prevents the stuck/jitter bug). (4) GHOSTS — they must be RENDERED and they must MOVE on their own. Three separate bugs to avoid: (a) ghosts in state but the draw loop never renders them ('no ghosts'); (b) ghosts drawn but FROZEN — the update loop never calls their move function, so they sit still and never chase (call updateGhost() for EVERY ghost EVERY frame, just like the player); (c) ghosts passing through walls — use the SAME wall-check as the player. Give each ghost a simple chase: each tick, pick the corridor-direction (not reversing) that reduces the tile distance to Pac-Man (greedy), or BFS for smarter chase. Verify ghost tile positions actually CHANGE over a second with no input. The harness flags PLAYER-STUCK when a movement key registers but the player position never changes — that means a wall/units bug, not a control-wiring bug. (5) ANIMATION — Pac-Man and ghosts must not look FROZEN: cycle `pacman_open`/`pacman_closed` via drawImage every ~100–120ms while `mode==='playing'` (not only when velocity is nonzero — classic chomp is continuous). Ghosts: drawImage each ghost_* / frightened key and add eye-look or a 1–2px bob so they read as alive while chasing. Never leave generated PNGs undrawn in favor of fill/arc when those keys exist (run_15 Pac-Man: ASSETS_LOADED_BUT_UNDRAWN on pacman_* and ghost_* while the on-screen figures looked frozen).

Grid-maze chase games (any theme — Pac-Man, Ms Pac-Man, pursuers instead of ghosts, etc.): after the player eats a power pickup, run a countdown timer (state.vulnerableTimer or state.powerTimer in frames/ms) that flips chaser AI to flee mode and allows eat-on-contact scoring. Expose the timer on window.state so probes can read it; per-chaser mode is fine too (mode in 'vulnerable','frightened','flee','scared'). When the timer hits zero, chasers resume normal chase. Do NOT hardcode ghost-only names — use state.chasers[] or state.enemies[] with the same wall checks as the player.

Quick-time-event (QTE) core loop: expose state.qte={active,prompt,windowStartMs,durationMs,result,expectKey} plus state.sceneEnteredAt. In update(now), derive windowOpen from now-sceneEnteredAt, but in keydown DO NOT trust a stale RAF-set flag: compute elapsed=performance.now()-state.sceneEnteredAt and openNow=elapsed>=q.windowStartMs && elapsed<=q.windowStartMs+q.durationMs. On every reaction keydown, set state.lastInputCode=e.code and state.inputFlash=150 so the harness sees visible response. If q.active && openNow && code===expectKey: result='success', score++, advance scene. If q.active && openNow && wrong reaction key: result='fail', lives--/retry. Use performance.now timing, not frame counts. Prompt must be 1-2 LARGE centered high-contrast words.

Cutscene/QTE scenes are image-first: each scene has a generated full-canvas background still (bg_scene) and optional generated sprite/pose frames. Draw bg with ctx.drawImage(ASSETS[scene.bg],0,0,W,H), then draw the knight/threat sprites; cycle generated frames at ~6-10 FPS for laserdisc cadence. Do NOT render the final scene as giant ctx.fillRect/arc procedural castle boxes when generated assets exist; procedural drawing is only a temporary missing-asset marker. Never code-draw limbs over sprites; the frames are the animation. Use short fade overlays for transitions.

For scripted scene/cutscene games, drive everything from deterministic SCENES=[{id,bg,frames,qte:{expectKey,prompt,windowStartMs,durationMs},onSuccess,onFail}]. state.scene is a ZERO-INDEXED array index; HUD displays state.scene+1. Probes must not require scene>=1 at page load. Restart (R) mutates state only: scene=0,lives=3,score=0,clear qte/flash, reset sceneEnteredAt; do not add new event listeners. Advance only on correct timed key or explicit non-QTE beat completion; wrong/missed QTE decrements lives and retries or game-over. Expose window.state and window.game={reset}.

For builder/management/strategy games (city-builder, space-trading, tycoon, idle): model the economy as state.resources={credits,food,…} updated on a fixed TICK (accumulate dt; every TICK_MS apply production-consumption), NOT per-frame — frame-rate-independent and lets you show per-tick deltas. Placing/buying validates affordability against resources, deducts cost, and registers a producer/consumer. Expose state.resources so probes can read economy changes. Show a resource HUD that updates each tick.

For lighting/darkness games (torch-lit dungeon, horror, fog-of-war): each frame fill an offscreen (or main) layer with a dark overlay, then 'punch' light by drawing radial gradients with globalCompositeOperation='destination-out' (or 'lighter' on a light layer) at the player and each light source; composite that layer over the scene. The gradient MUST fade to transparent at its edge (addColorStop(1,'rgba(0,0,0,0)')) for soft falloff — a hard-edged circle or a fully-bright screen is the failure mode. Light radius follows the player; torches flicker by jittering radius.

For drag-and-drop games (cards/solitaire, tile/match, board pieces, inventory): on pointerdown hit-test the topmost draggable under the cursor and store it as state.dragging with a grab-offset; on pointermove move it with the cursor (draw it LAST so it floats above); on pointerup hit-test the drop target, validate the move rule, and either commit (move the item into the target collection) or snap back to origin. Map clientX/Y through canvas.getBoundingClientRect(). Expose the dragging/target state so probes can confirm a pick changes state and a legal drop relocates the item.

Temporary control-blocking states (hit-stun, knockdown, charge, freeze) MUST tick their expiry timer BEFORE the early-return guard that blocks input during the state. `if (p.action === 'hit') return;` placed ABOVE `p.actionTimer -= dt` makes the lock PERMANENT — the timer never decrements, the player never regains control after one hit. Pattern: decrement the timer first, clear the state when it reaches 0 (`p.actionTimer -= dt; if (p.actionTimer <= 0) p.action = 'idle';`), THEN early-return while still in the state. Applies to any entity state machine with timed states.

Sprite facing is a CODE convention, not something the asset pipeline fixes for you. AUTHOR every directional character/pose as ONE right-facing sprite set (idle, walk, kick, ...) and FLIP in code to face left: ctx.save(); ctx.translate(x+w/2, y+h/2); ctx.scale(-1,1); ctx.drawImage(sprite, -w/2, -h/2, w, h); ctx.restore(). Do NOT name sprites by screen direction (no hero_left/hero_right, dodge_left/dodge_right) and do NOT generate a separate mirrored PNG per direction -- one pose plus a code flip is the whole mechanism. CAUTION with diffusion prompts: the image generator often INVERTS 'facing right'/'facing left' (it draws the character's right side, i.e. the body pointing screen-LEFT), so do NOT rely on those words in <assets> prompts -- describe the pose, and correct any backward art with a one-line ctx.scale(-1,1) at draw time, never by regenerating. Derive facing ONCE from the target's position -- facing = Math.sign(target.x - x) || 1 -- and reuse that SAME variable for the sprite flip, the melee hitbox offset (x + facing*reach), and the projectile vx (facing*speed): a mismatch makes attacks render or hit backward.

QTE timing must be READABLE FROM THE PICTURE, not just the prompt word. Give each scene/room a hazardPath:{from,to,threatens:'head/body/feet/sword'} plus cueMs, windowStartMs/durationMs, hitMs. Telegraph in 3 phases off elapsed=performance.now()-sceneEnteredAt: (1) cue/windup ~300-700ms before the window with OBVIOUS anticipation (axe rears back, floor cracks, dragon inhales, tentacle coils) — never 'subtle'; (2) during the window the threat is visibly closing in; (3) at hitMs it reaches the threatened part. Drive the live threat position every frame with pointOnPath(hazardPath, t01) where t01=(elapsed-cueMs)/(hitMs-cueMs) clamped 0..1 (ease it for anticipation), so the threat sprite physically approaches the threatened body part. On a correct reaction flip the hero pose to dodge that part BEFORE impact. The big centered word only says WHICH key; the moving threat says WHEN.

A character that recurs across multiple poses, multiple scene backgrounds, AND cutscene videos stays on-model only if you lock its identity with ONE canonical description. Define the character once (build, exact palette/costume, distinguishing marks, art-style clause) and reuse that string VERBATIM as the LEADING clause of every asset prompt that contains the character — idle + each pose, changing ONLY the pose clause. The pipeline's fixed seed (42) + identical description renders the SAME character every time (proven mechanism — see animation-frames-consistent-character; img2img/from_image cannot change a pose, only txt2img + shared description moves limbs). Keep characters OUT of background art: bg_* are EMPTY stages, and the character only ever appears as its transparent sprite drawn OVER the bg — baking a character into a background yields a second, drifted copy that breaks scene-to-scene consistency. For cutscenes, generate ONE key-art still using the SAME canonical description, then seed EVERY video as image-to-video from that still (set 'image' to the key-art asset name) — text-to-video drifts off-model. Net: idle, poses, every scene, and every cutscene all read as one character.

Endless runner / flappy / auto-scroller: the WORLD scrolls past a fixed-x player, not the player moving forward. Drive everything off one scrollSpeed that ramps with distance/score. The player has ONE input (jump/flap/lane-swap); on keydown/pointerdown set vy = -FLAP (flappy) or trigger a jump if grounded (runner). Spawn obstacles/gaps off the right edge on a distance- or time-interval, push them left by scrollSpeed*dt, and SPLICE them once off the left edge — a finite pre-placed level ends the 'endless'. Procedurally pick each gap's size/height within a playable band (never narrower than the player can clear at current speed). Score = distance survived or gaps passed. Collision = AABB player vs each obstacle every frame. Common bugs: moving the player right instead of scrolling the world (camera drifts off), forgetting to despawn (array grows until GC stutter), ramping speed past the human reaction limit (cap it).

Box-pushing puzzles (Sokoban / crate-push / block-slide): the player can PUSH a box only into an EMPTY floor cell, and only ONE box at a time (never push a box into another box or a wall). Movement check, in order: compute the target cell in the pressed direction; if it is a wall, do nothing; if it holds a box, look at the cell BEYOND the box — push only if that beyond-cell is empty floor (move both box and player); else the cell is empty floor and the player simply steps. Store boxes either as a value in grid[r][c] or as a boxes[] list, but pick ONE representation and test/mutate it consistently. Win when every goal/target cell is covered by a box. Add an undo stack (snapshot positions per move) because deadlocks are common. Bug to avoid: pushing a box into a corner or against another box because the beyond-cell check was skipped.

Hidden-grid deduction games (Minesweeper / flood-reveal / tap-to-clear): store grid[r][c] with {mine, revealed, flagged, adjacent}. FIRST-CLICK-SAFE: place mines AFTER the first click, excluding that cell (and ideally its neighbors), so the player never dies on move one. Precompute each non-mine cell's adjacent-mine count once. Reveal is a FLOOD FILL: on revealing a cell with adjacent===0, recursively (or via a queue/stack to avoid stack overflow on big boards) reveal its 8 neighbors, stopping flood at numbered (adjacent>0) cells. Right-click / long-press toggles a flag (and must preventDefault the context menu). Lose on revealing a mine; win when all non-mine cells are revealed. Bugs: recursive flood without a visited/early-return guard infinite-loops; counting adjacency at draw time instead of once at init; first click hitting a mine.

Gravity-drop column boards (Connect Four / Drop Four / token-stack): the player picks a COLUMN, not a cell; the token falls to the LOWEST empty row in that column. Find the landing row by scanning that column from the bottom up for the first empty slot (or track a per-column height counter). Reject a move on a full column. Win check after each drop: from the placed cell, scan the 4 directions (horizontal, vertical, both diagonals) counting same-color tokens each way and summing — a run of N (4 for Connect Four) wins; do NOT rescan the whole board. Draw when the board fills with no winner. Alternate turns only on a successful drop. Optional drop animation: animate the token's y from the top to the landing row before committing visually. Bug: placing in the clicked row instead of the gravity-settled row.

Deckbuilder / card-battler loop (Slay-the-Spire-like): keep FOUR card zones as arrays — drawPile, hand, discardPile, exhaust — plus an energy budget per turn. Turn start: refill energy, draw N cards (move from drawPile to hand). Playing a card costs energy, applies its effect, then moves it to discardPile (or exhaust). When drawPile empties on a draw, SHUFFLE discardPile back into drawPile and continue — forgetting this is the #1 bug (you run out of cards forever). End turn: discard the remaining hand, run enemy intents, then start the next turn. Validate energy BEFORE playing; block play at 0. Expose the zone lengths on state so probes can confirm a play moves a card hand->discard and energy decreases. Keep card definitions as data {cost, type, effect} and resolve effects through one apply function, not per-card branches scattered in the UI.

Persisting progress (high scores, save slots, settings, idle-game state): serialize a plain snapshot of state to JSON and store it under a single namespaced key (localStorage.setItem('mygame.save', JSON.stringify(snapshot))). ALWAYS feature-detect + try/catch both read and write — some embedded/headless contexts throw SecurityError on access (see no-localstorage-init). Include a version field in the snapshot and, on load, migrate or discard mismatched/corrupt data (wrap JSON.parse in try/catch; fall back to defaults) so a bad blob never crashes startup. Save on meaningful events (level up, game over, settings change) and/or a throttled interval — NOT every frame. Only persist serializable state (numbers, strings, plain arrays/objects); never store DOM nodes, Image objects, or functions. Provide an explicit reset that clears the key.

Difficulty / progression scaling (waves, levels, depth, speed-up): drive difficulty from ONE monotonic progress variable (wave number, score, distance, floor depth, elapsed time) and derive concrete parameters from it with simple formulas — spawnInterval = base * Math.pow(0.92, wave), enemySpeed = base + wave*step, enemyHp = Math.ceil(base * (1 + 0.15*wave)). Clamp every derived value to a sane floor/ceiling so late game stays playable, not impossible (min spawn interval, max speed below the human reaction limit). Advance the progress variable on a clear trigger (all enemies cleared, score threshold, timer) and surface it in the HUD so the player feels escalation. Avoid hardcoding per-level constants in a giant switch; a formula scales to unlimited levels and is easy to tune. Keep early levels gentle (the first 2-3 should be winnable while learning).

Help screens, tutorials, instructions, how-to-play panels, HUD/menu overlays, and credits are UI/code features. Implement them as DOM overlays or canvas/dialog panels with a <patch>; do NOT emit <assets> unless the user explicitly asks for new artwork. If the user combines 'add a help screen' with a game-logic question ('how do I dig/use/open X?'), add the help UI and verify the described interaction path in code instead of regenerating sprites/backgrounds.

When the user asks for a help/hint/HUD/menu feature AND the failing probes are checking for exactly that UI (e.g. #helpBtn present, #hintText visible, help overlay toggles), the user request IS the harness fix - patch the DOM/handlers this turn, do not defer it behind the blocker. A cosmetic asset/animation warning never outranks implementing the UI the probes require. Add the element with a stable id matching the probe selector and a key/button toggle, then re-run.

Star Wars-style trench vector runs: spawn obstacles/towers/TIE wireframes at large z (far away), each frame decrease z toward the camera so lines grow outward from the horizon. Project both endpoints of each segment with the same perspective divide-by-rz formula; scale apparent width by 1/rz. Remove obstacles once z passes behind the player. Crosshair stays fixed at screen center; lateral player.x shifts the trench view left/right.

Side-scroll beat-em-ups: ONE hero with camera.x following player.x; enemies[] spawn off-screen at camera.x + canvas.width + margin and walk toward the hero. Facing flips from vx sign only — do NOT copy the two-player fighting state machine (players[2]). Punch/kick hitboxes active only on attack frames with a per-enemy hasHit flag.

Chess/holochess ANIMATION IS THE PRODUCT — block clicks while state.animating. On a legal move: tween drawX/drawY square-by-square along the path while cycling walk frames on a timer (~120ms); knights use a hop arc; commit board[] ONLY when the tween ends — never teleport. On capture: TWO beats BEFORE removing the victim — (1) lift capturer holding victim overhead, (2) slam victim down — then clear the cell. Castling slides both pieces; promotion plays a brief transform; check shakes/glows the king. Expose state.animating, state.capturePhase, and moving draw coords for probes.

Chess/strategy board games with a CPU opponent: human is White/Light and moves first; computer plays Black/Dark (enable AI on by default). Search at least 3 plies with negamax + alpha-beta on a material eval — NOT a 1-ply greedy capture grab. Root move + depth-2 search = 3 plies. Block human input on CPU's turn; schedule AI after each human move.

Tower-defense enemies must advance along the path by index, not freestyle pixels: track {pathIdx, t} per enemy and interpolate position between path[pathIdx] and path[pathIdx+1] (FIXED PATH = pre-drawn waypoints; OPEN FIELD = BFS path from spawn to exit). Steering by raw dx/dy toward the exit makes creeps cut corners and walk through towers.

Tower-defense turrets must enforce a per-tower cooldown timer (e.g. tower.cooldown -= dt; fire only when <=0 then reset to fireInterval). Firing every frame deletes enemies instantly and looks like a solid beam. Re-validate the target is still alive AND in range on each shot before spawning a projectile.

OPEN-FIELD / Fieldrunners tower defense (no pre-drawn path): towers BUILD THE MAZE. Before committing a placement, run BFS from entrance to exit on the grid with the new tower marked blocked — REJECT the placement if it walls off the exit (no path found). After any accepted placement, RE-PATH in-flight creeps so they route around the new maze instead of freezing or clipping through.

Rotating turrets ONLY (gun/missile/goo/mortar — NOT Tesla/Flame): draw a fixed base sprite + a separate HEAD sprite chosen from ~5 pre-rendered angles (E/SE/S/NE/N, mirror for west) and lerp a displayAngle toward the target — do NOT ctx.rotate a single turret PNG (smears pixels, looks wrong). Add recoil/muzzle-flash on fire. Generate multi-angle head frames, not one rotating image.

Tower-defense beam towers (Tesla/Flame) are NOT rotatable turrets: draw the tower as a fixed vertical/upright sprite — never lerp head angles or ctx.rotate the body. While a target is in range, render a PROCEDURAL attack (lightning bolt with sparks / flame jet) from the tower top TO the enemy each frame; only the beam endpoint tracks the creep, not the tower sprite.

Tower-defense waves must stagger spawns with an interval timer (spawnTimer -= dt; spawn next enemy when <=0) — never spawn a whole wave in one frame (a burst stacks creeps on one tile and overwhelms instantly). Escalate later waves (more hp / tougher types). A wave ends only when all its enemies are dead or leaked; then schedule the next, stronger wave.

Dragon's Lair QTE default template = 8 classic scenes (Drawbridge JUMP, Slime SWORD, Blade DUCK+JUMP, Stairs RIGHT+JUMP, Bats SWORD+DUCK, Lava LEFT+JUMP, Throne RIGHT+SWORD, Dragon LEFT+SWORD) — use ONLY when the user's goal does not specify scene count or perils. If the goal asks for N scenes, cartoon style, different characters, or new traps, follow the goal: same hazard-on-bg telegraph rule for every scene, but scene list, art prompts, and asset count must match what the user asked for, not this template.

USER GOAL WINS over curated memory: playbooks, implementation outlines, visual playtests, skeletons, and prompt-library entries are reference patterns and defaults — helpful starting points, not a freeze on your build. Read the user's goal (and later feedback) first. When they specify scene count, art style, characters, mechanics, roster size, level count, or pacing that differs from a retrieved recipe, FOLLOW THE GOAL: keep underlying mechanism lessons (state on window, RAF, inputFlash, hazard telegraph, patch-not-rewrite) but change counts, names, asset prompts, and criteria to match what they asked for. When the goal is vague, use the best-matching template as a strong default. On feedback turns, the latest user message overrides stale plan/criteria from earlier turns.

In top-down canvas shooters, player craft, enemy swarm, and projectile sprites must agree on ONE world orientation at draw time — the nose/front points in the direction the entity moves or fires. Asset prompts may describe facing in words; if the PNG faces the wrong way, fix with draw-time ctx.rotate / ctx.scale BEFORE regenerating. Verify rendered pixels: player shots exit the front/nose (not the tail), enemies in the upper playfield face downward toward the player, and the player ship at the bottom faces upward. Numeric `facing` probes alone can pass while sprites are backward. Fixed-shooter guns/ships: generate art with the muzzle/nose pointing UP the PNG; fire with vy<0 so shots leave the front, not the tail.

When generated PNGs exist, every entity with a sprite MUST be drawn via drawImage — never a steady-state fillRect/arc circle/box. Pattern: `const ASSETS = {}; async function loadAssets(){ for (const [k,p] of Object.entries(PATHS)){ const img=new Image(); img.src=p; ASSETS[k]=img; try{await img.decode();}catch(e){} } } function sprite(key){ return ASSETS[key]||null; } function drawEntity(key,x,y,w,h){ const img=sprite(key); if(img&&img.complete&&img.naturalWidth>0){ ctx.drawImage(img,x-w/2,y-h/2,w,h); } else { ctx.fillStyle='#f0f'; ctx.fillRect(x-w/2,y-h/2,w,h); ctx.fillStyle='#000'; ctx.font='10px monospace'; ctx.fillText('MISSING:'+key,x-w/2,y); } }` Start RAF immediately; loadAssets() runs in the background. When every decode/onload completes, set window._assetsReady = true (harness asset-settle poll). loadAssets() alone is insufficient — each entity draw path must call drawImage when the image is ready. Procedural shapes are ONLY for entities with NO generated PNG (HUD bars, grid lines, particles) or as the labeled MISSING fallback while decoding.

The browser harness tests movement by dispatching keydown (e.g. ArrowRight) and checking state.player.x changes. Prior keydowns in the same probe session can leave the player in crouch, duck, block, or attack states. Do NOT gate horizontal movement ONLY on `idle || walk` — include crouch/duck/block-walk OR reset `action` to walk/idle before applying dx when a move key is held. Otherwise `input_moves_player` false-fails while the game works for a human. Same rule for any action-state machine: movement keys must work from every locomotion-capable state the harness might enter.

Games with generated `<videos>` intros (floor intro, boss entrance): startup MUST reliably enter the playable phase and spawn enemies/waves — use the SAME `reset()` or `startGame()` path as the R restart key. Do NOT rely on a one-off `setTimeout` that only runs when `phase==='cutscene_intro'`; if phase drifts or the timer never fires, enemies never spawn, boss sprites stay undrawn, and ASSETS_LOADED_BUT_UNDRAWN fires. Wire video `onended`/`onerror`/skip-key to the same continuation callback that sets `phase='play'` and calls `startWave(1)` (or equivalent).

three.js minimap / radar facing arrow ONLY — NOT for 2D wireframe canvas (see wireframe-minimap-radar-yaw-arrow). Compute forward ONCE with the SAME basis as movement. PREFERRED: `const dir = new THREE.Vector3(); camera.getWorldDirection(dir); const fx = dir.x, fz = dir.z;` OR manual fallback matching camera: `fx = -Math.sin(yaw); fz = -Math.cos(yaw)`. Map world (x,z) to canvas (px,pz). Draw facing line to `(px + fx * len, pz + fz * len)`. NEVER flip minimap alone when the 3D view disagrees — fix the shared movement/camera basis first (see 3d-navigation-modality-invariants). NEVER hand-roll `+sin/-cos` variants that diverge from movement.

2D canvas wireframe first-person (Battlezone / tank / trench) on the xz plane — NOT three.js. Use ONE yaw basis for turn, move, projection, and radar. Turn: `yaw -= turnRate*dt` on ArrowLeft (or += on ArrowRight). Forward: `x += Math.sin(yaw)*speed; z += Math.cos(yaw)*speed` — note +cos on z, NOT three.js `fz = -Math.cos(yaw)`. Backward subtract the same pair. Strafe-right: `x += Math.cos(yaw)*speed; z -= Math.sin(yaw)*speed`. Projection already rotates with `cos(-yaw), sin(-yaw)`. Radar/minimap arrows use the same sin/cos as movement — see wireframe-minimap-radar-yaw-arrow. NEVER paste three.js `-cos(z)` from fps-camera-and-movement-vectors into wireframe code — it inverts forward/strafe on some axes.

Textured first-person mazes: use three.js CDN (camera + renderer) — do not hand-roll canvas DDA. Navigation invariants (any modality): (1) movement forward matches look forward (dot≈1 on xz); if you did ship a canvas raycaster, castRay (dx,dz) MUST reuse the WASD forward pair and fisheye is depth*cos(rayAngle-yaw). (2) Minimap/radar/fire reuse that same vector; draw a small overview minimap. (3) three.js move: applyQuaternion(camera.quaternion) or fx=-sin(yaw), fz=-cos(yaw); wireframe canvas: x+=sin(yaw), z+=cos(yaw) (+cos z). Classic failure: walk/gun axis opposite look (backwards facing).

2D wireframe canvas minimap / radar facing arrow ONLY — NOT for three.js WebGL (see fps-minimap-radar-yaw-arrow). Use the SAME basis as wireframe movement: `fx = Math.sin(yaw); fz = Math.cos(yaw)` (+cos on z). Map world (x,z) to canvas (px,pz). Draw facing line to `(px + fx * len, pz + fz * len)`. Mouse/right turn should rotate the arrow clockwise on the radar when yaw increases with ArrowRight. NEVER paste three.js `-cos(z)` here.

Circle-vs-circle or circle-vs-bumper resolve: (1) compute overlap = (r1+r2) - dist; if overlap <= 0 skip; (2) push ball along normal by overlap + 1px BEFORE reflecting; (3) reflect velocity: v -= 2*(v·n)*n; add optional impulse along n; (4) set hitCooldown[id] = 3-5 frames so overlapping bodies cannot re-score every frame — stops bumper ping-pong loops. One hit per body per frame is the minimum bar.

Charged launch (plunger, cannon, slingshot exit) must enter the playfield with BOTH vertical AND horizontal velocity — pure vertical (vy only, vx≈0) traps the body in a launch lane or makes it bounce straight back. Pattern: on release set vy to target speed (600-900 px/s for pinball) AND vx toward table center (±150-250). Angled guide walls at the shooter mouth help curve the body into the main area. Probe: speed after release increases and position leaves the shooter zone. Launch-lane trap: separator wall must stop BELOW the exit gap; guide curve must not share the same corner as the lane mouth or the body bounces back / exits off the right edge. FORBIDDEN on plunger release: `ball.vx = 0` (even with a comment claiming leftward vx). REQUIRED when the table is LEFT of the launch lane: `ball.vy = -(600 + p*300); ball.vx = -180 - p*70;` (scale p in 0..1 from charge). FORBIDDEN while still inLane before the exit gap: `vx = Math.abs(vx)` (or similar) on the playfield-side wall — that cancels table-ward launch; constrain x only, or clear inLane when y is above the separator gap. Do not invent max-height algebra — ship the snippet then patch geometry. FORBIDDEN: set `inLane=false` on plunger release while the ball is still below the separator gap — keep inLane true until `y` clears the gap, then release into the table.

Flipper / paddle segment collision is NOT a static wall: when the segment angle is rising (angular velocity > 0), add extra upward impulse along the contact normal (400-700 px/s boost band) so the body lifts into play. Static reflection-only flippers feel dead; rising flipper must transfer energy. Decrement flipper angle on release quickly; contact while rising is the bat window.
keydown auto-repeat double-fires actions: track keys[code], act on press edge only
update and draw reading different state copies: one window.state, everything reads it
row/col vs x/y mixups: store board[row][col], convert to pixels only at draw boundaries
input accepted while animating or AI thinking: block with state.animating
drawImage before decode finishes: blank or flickering sprites - gate the loop on load
code-drawn shapes layered over loaded sprites: pick ONE representation per entity
moving then checking walls: validate the target tile first or the player enters walls
pixel coords stored in state: keep tile coords (tx,ty), derive px=tx*TILE only at draw
jump allowed mid-air: require onGround, set it false immediately on jump
landing without zeroing vy: snap y = platform.y - player.h AND vy = 0 or it jitters
fx=+sin(yaw) with camera.rotation.y=yaw: gun points opposite movement on world X
forward sign differs between movement and camera: ONE convention everywhere
bullets spawn every held frame: fire on cooldown or keydown edge, not key state
diagonal movement 41% faster: divide vx,vy by sqrt(2) when both axes pressed
rotating without re-checking collision overlaps walls: test the rotated shape first
clearing rows top-down while splicing skips rows: scan and remove bottom-up
drawing road front-to-back: paint far segments first or nothing layers correctly
steering active at zero speed: scale steering by speed/maxSpeed
ball trapped inside paddle: bounce only when vy>0 AND reposition ball above the paddle
two bricks hit in one frame double-reverse vy: break after the first brick hit
holding Up crosses in one frame: move one row per keydown edge, not key state
player stored in rows but collision in pixels: convert once, compare in one unit
raw clientX/Y on a CSS-scaled canvas: always map through the bounding rect
hit-testing a stale hotspot list after scene change: read the active scene list each click
origin offset missing: sx=(col-row)*TILE_W/2 needs +canvas.width/2 or all is off-screen left
drawing in array order not (row+col) order: depth glitches - sort or iterate diagonally
free pixel movement: commit one tile per input and snap - no in-between resting states
Action checks the player's own tile: test the tile the player FACES
ladder yTop/yBot hardcoded in pixels: DERIVE from platform.y values or they drift apart
hazard spawner runs once: it must be a repeating loop that keeps barrels coming
timer decrement below `if action==='hit' return`: the hit player NEVER recovers - tick timers before any early-return
facing derived in 2+ places: drift makes kicks land backwards - one derivation, draw-time flip only
two-player fighting state machine copied: use enemies[] not players[2]
enemies spawn on-screen on top of hero: x = camera.x + canvas.width + margin
unbounded particle growth kills FPS: hard cap (drop oldest) every frame
splice inside a forward loop skips neighbors: iterate backwards
economy run per frame: use a fixed tick (e.g. 1/s) or numbers explode with FPS
placement ignoring occupancy: track building presence in tiles[][]
stranding: never allow a jump that leaves no reachable refuel - check before commit
